h1Title: Acute Sinusitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute sinusitis is inflammation of the mucous membranes lining one or more of the paranasal sinuses, lasting fewer than four weeks. It is one of the most frequently diagnosed conditions in primary care, accounting for approximately 31 million physician visits per year in the United States. Viral upper respiratory tract infections trigger roughly 90-98% of cases; bacterial superinfection develops in only 0.5-2% of viral episodes. Symptoms include facial pain or pressure, nasal obstruction, purulent discharge, and reduced sense of smell. Most episodes resolve without antibiotics within ten days. The IDSA 2012 guidelines recommend watchful waiting for uncomplicated cases, reserving amoxicillin-clavulanate for confirmed bacterial sinusitis.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 31 million cases diagnosed annually in the United States (CDC); estimated 1 in 8 US adults affected each year
keyStats.demographics: Most prevalent in adults aged 25-64; women slightly more affected than men; higher rates in smokers and those with allergic rhinitis
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in adults aged 30-50; children have 6-8 upper respiratory infections per year, many involving the sinuses
keyStats.globalCases: Sinusitis ranks among the top five conditions for which antibiotics are prescribed globally; estimated direct US healthcare costs exceed $11 billion annually

definition: Acute sinusitis (ICD-10: J01.90) is an inflammatory disorder of the paranasal sinuses — maxillary, frontal, ethmoid, or sphenoid — with symptom duration under four weeks. The condition falls under the broader category of rhinosinusitis because concurrent nasal mucosal inflammation is almost universal. Pathophysiologically, viral infection or allergic inflammation causes mucosal edema that obstructs the sinus ostia, impairs mucociliary clearance, and creates a low-oxygen environment conducive to bacterial proliferation. The maxillary sinuses are most commonly affected, followed by the ethmoid sinuses. Acute sinusitis is classified by aetiology — viral, bacterial, or allergic — and by the number of affected sinuses. Bacterial acute sinusitis is distinguished clinically by worsening after initial improvement (double worsening), symptoms persisting beyond ten days without improvement, or onset with severe symptoms including fever above 39°C and purulent discharge. Otolaryngology and primary care physicians manage this condition, with ENT referral warranted for recurrent episodes, orbital or intracranial complications, or failure of two antibiotic courses.

typesClassification.description: Caused by rhinovirus (most common), influenza, parainfluenza, or coronavirus. Accounts for 90-98% of acute sinusitis episodes. Symptoms peak at 48-72 hours and typically improve within 7-10 days without antibiotic therapy.

Streptococcus pneumoniae (20-43%), Haemophilus influenzae (22-35%), and Moraxella catarrhalis (2-10%) are the dominant pathogens. Clinically suspected when symptoms persist beyond 10 days, worsen after initial improvement, or are severe at onset with fever and unilateral facial pain.

Driven by IgE-mediated mucosal inflammation from aeroallergen exposure (grass, dust mite, mold). Tends to be bilateral, recurrent, and associated with sneezing and clear discharge rather than purulence. Overlaps with chronic sinusitis without polyps.

Four or more episodes per year, each lasting 7-30 days with symptom-free intervals. Suggests an underlying structural (deviated septum, polyps) or immunological predisposition warranting ENT evaluation.

primarySymptoms: Facial pain or pressure over the affected sinus — maxillary sinusitis produces cheek pain that may radiate to the upper teeth; frontal sinusitis causes forehead pain worsened by leaning forward.
Nasal congestion or obstruction making breathing through the nose difficult, often worse when lying down, present in virtually all cases.
Purulent (thick, discolored) nasal discharge anteriorly or posteriorly draining into the throat, distinguishing moderate-to-severe cases from simple viral rhinitis.
Reduced or absent sense of smell (hyposmia or anosmia) from edema blocking the olfactory cleft, reported by 60-70% of patients during an acute episode.
Headache that is typically dull and pressure-like, worsened by bending forward or Valsalva maneuver, and localized to the affected sinus region.
Postnasal drip producing a persistent cough, throat clearing, or globus sensation, particularly troublesome at night and first thing in the morning.
Fever, which is present in 25-50% of viral cases and more consistently in bacterial sinusitis, occasionally exceeding 39°C in true bacterial acute rhinosinusitis.
Fatigue and general malaise proportional to the severity of infection, persisting through the active inflammatory phase.
Dental pain in the upper molar region, particularly with maxillary sinusitis, because the roots of upper posterior teeth lie close to or within the maxillary sinus floor.
Ear fullness or mild pressure from Eustachian tube dysfunction associated with nasopharyngeal inflammation.
earlyWarningSigns: Unilateral facial pain that begins to worsen after initial symptom improvement on day 5-6 of a cold, suggesting bacterial superinfection rather than straightforward viral rhinitis.
Increasing facial swelling around the eye or over one cheek during a sinus infection, which may indicate preseptal cellulitis or early orbital spread.
Persistent fever above 38.5°C lasting more than 72 hours without improvement on symptomatic therapy.
Thick, foul-smelling, unilateral purulent discharge — particularly from a single nostril — which can indicate dental-origin maxillary sinusitis or a foreign body in children.
Significant worsening after initial improvement (double-worsening pattern) on or after day 5-6 of symptoms.
emergencySigns: Periorbital swelling with proptosis, restricted eye movement, or double vision — orbital cellulitis or abscess requires urgent CT imaging and IV antibiotics or surgical drainage.
Severe frontal headache with photophobia, neck stiffness, or altered consciousness — intracranial spread including meningitis, subdural empyema, or cavernous sinus thrombosis is a medical emergency.
High fever (above 39°C) with signs of sepsis (tachycardia, hypotension, confusion) in a patient with known sinusitis — bacteraemia requires immediate IV antibiotics and hospital admission.
Sudden change in visual acuity or new diplopia during sinusitis — requires same-day ophthalmology and ENT assessment.
Forehead swelling with doughy consistency (Pott's puffy tumor) indicating frontal bone osteomyelitis from frontal sinusitis.

causes.description: Rhinovirus, influenza, and coronavirus cause mucosal edema that obstructs sinus ostia. Approximately 0.5-2% of viral colds progress to bacterial superinfection. This is by far the dominant mechanism, accounting for 90-98% of acute sinusitis cases worldwide.

When viral-induced ostial obstruction persists, the stagnant mucus and low-oxygen environment allow colonizing bacteria — primarily Streptococcus pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenzae — to multiply and invade the sinus mucosa, causing bacterial acute rhinosinusitis (ABRS).

IgE-mediated mucosal inflammation from seasonal or perennial allergens (pollen, dust mite, mold) produces edema and increased mucus secretion that can obstruct sinus drainage, predisposing to both viral and bacterial sinusitis. Allergic rhinitis is present in 20-40% of patients with recurrent sinusitis.

Periapical abscesses or dental procedures involving upper molar roots can directly inoculate the maxillary sinus, causing predominantly anaerobic bacterial infection. Odontogenic sinusitis accounts for 10-40% of unilateral maxillary sinusitis cases and commonly fails standard antibiotics that don't cover anaerobes.

A deviated nasal septum, nasal polyps, turbinate hypertrophy, or a concha bullosa (pneumatized middle turbinate) can narrow the osteomeatal complex, impairing mucociliary drainage and predisposing to recurrent sinusitis. These structural factors are identified in 30-40% of patients requiring sinus surgery.

Cigarette smoke, dry air, air pollution, and chemical irritants impair mucociliary clearance and increase susceptibility. Swimming in chlorinated pools and air travel with nasal congestion (causing barotrauma-related ostial closure) are acute precipitants.

riskFactors.description: Present in 20-40% of sinusitis patients; uncontrolled mucosal inflammation from allergens persistently narrows sinus ostia and increases viral susceptibility. Allergen immunotherapy and intranasal corticosteroids reduce sinusitis frequency.

Active smoking impairs mucociliary function, increases colonization with S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae, and approximately doubles sinusitis risk. Passive smoke exposure in children increases ENT infections significantly.

riskFactors.factor: Deviated nasal septum or nasal polyps
riskFactors.description: Structural obstruction of the osteomeatal complex impairs drainage. Nasal polyps are found in 20-30% of patients with recurrent or chronic sinusitis and are associated with aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease.

HIV, common variable immunodeficiency, IgA deficiency, and neutropenia are associated with recurrent bacterial sinusitis from impaired bacterial clearance. Sinusitis refractory to two antibiotic courses should prompt immunological evaluation.

The strongest immediate risk factor; 99% of bacterial sinusitis episodes are preceded by a viral cold. Day-care attendance in children and frequent social contact in adults increase exposure frequency.

Barotrauma from cabin pressure changes during ascent and descent can block sinus ostia, particularly in passengers with nasal congestion, causing acute sinus pain (aerosinusitis) and occasionally bacterial secondary infection.

Asthma and sinusitis co-occur in 20-40% of patients through shared airway inflammation pathways. Sinusitis exacerbates asthma control, and treating sinusitis improves asthma outcomes in about 70% of co-affected patients.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 25-64 are most commonly affected; women experience acute sinusitis at slightly higher rates than men (CDC National Health Interview Survey).
Children aged 2-6 years have high rates of acute sinusitis because of frequent viral upper respiratory infections (6-8 per year) and immature sinus pneumatization.
Smokers and those with allergic rhinitis have approximately twice the annual sinusitis risk of the general non-smoking, non-allergic population.
Immunocompromised patients (HIV, organ transplant, chemotherapy) are at increased risk of recurrent and atypical sinusitis from opportunistic organisms including fungal pathogens.
diagnosisOverview: Acute sinusitis is a clinical diagnosis in most cases. The IDSA 2012 and AAO-HNS 2023 guidelines recommend diagnosing bacterial acute rhinosinusitis (ABRS) based on three clinical criteria: symptoms persisting beyond 10 days without improvement, severe onset with fever above 39°C and purulent nasal discharge or facial pain lasting at least 3-4 days, or onset with worsening after initial improvement (double-worsening pattern). Physical examination may reveal tenderness on palpation over the maxillary or frontal sinuses, erythema, or purulent discharge visible in the middle meatus. Anterior rhinoscopy or nasal endoscopy identifies purulence at the middle meatus and excludes polyps or structural obstruction. Imaging is not routinely recommended for uncomplicated acute sinusitis. CT of the paranasal sinuses is reserved for cases refractory to two antibiotic courses, recurrent episodes (to identify structural cause), or suspected complications such as orbital or intracranial extension. Plain sinus radiographs have low sensitivity and specificity and are no longer recommended. Cultures of nasal secretions are unreliable; endoscopically guided middle meatal culture or maxillary sinus puncture is the reference standard for bacterial identification but is reserved for immunocompromised patients, treatment failures, or clinical trials.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes diagnosis of ABRS using IDSA symptom criteria: persistence beyond 10 days, severe onset, or double-worsening pattern
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician asks about symptom onset, duration, and character; examines the nose with a light or rhinoscope; palpates the sinuses; and checks for neck stiffness. Takes about 10-15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualizes purulent discharge at the middle meatus confirming sinus origin; identifies polyps, septal deviation, or other structural abnormality
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small light-tipped scope is passed through the nostril. Nasal endoscopy may cause brief mild discomfort; local decongestant spray is often applied beforehand. Takes 5 minutes in clinic.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive anatomical imaging for surgical planning, recurrent sinusitis workup, and exclusion of complications
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-minute scan lying still in the CT tube. No contrast injection needed. Results available within an hour. Exposes patients to a low radiation dose equivalent to 5-10 chest X-rays.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Helps distinguish viral from bacterial sinusitis; CRP above 10 mg/L combined with symptom duration supports ABRS and antibiotic prescribing in guideline-based algorithms
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A finger-prick or standard venous blood draw. Results within 10-20 minutes for point-of-care testing. Does not confirm the diagnosis alone but is useful in borderline cases.

diagnosticTests.test: Allergy testing (skin prick test or specific IgE)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies allergic sensitization in patients with recurrent sinusitis overlapping with allergic rhinitis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Skin prick tests take 20 minutes (small drops of allergens placed on the forearm, read at 15 min). Blood tests for specific IgE take 3-5 days for results. Helpful for long-term management planning.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies causative bacteria and antibiotic sensitivities in treatment-refractory or immunocompromised patients
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed by an ENT specialist. A sterile swab is passed under endoscopic guidance to the middle meatus. Mild nasal discomfort. Culture results take 2-3 days.

treatmentOverview: The majority of acute sinusitis cases are viral and resolve without antibiotics. The cornerstone of management is symptomatic relief: intranasal saline irrigation (isotonic or hypertonic), intranasal corticosteroids, analgesics, and decongestants. The IDSA 2012 and NICE 2017 guidelines recommend watchful waiting for 10 days in uncomplicated presentations, with a safety-net antibiotic prescription to be filled if symptoms worsen or do not improve within 7 days. When bacterial acute rhinosinusitis is confirmed by clinical criteria, amoxicillin-clavulanate (875/125 mg twice daily for 5-7 days in adults) is first-line per IDSA guidance, replacing plain amoxicillin given the rise in beta-lactamase-producing H. influenzae and M. catarrhalis. Doxycycline or a respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin, moxifloxacin) is used in penicillin-allergic patients. Duration of therapy is 5-7 days for adults and 10-14 days for children. Clinical response should occur within 72 hours of starting antibiotics; failure to improve warrants a change in antibiotic class, cultures, or CT imaging. Intranasal corticosteroids (mometasone, fluticasone) are recommended as monotherapy for mild ABRS and as adjuncts to antibiotics in moderate-severe disease; they reduce symptom severity and duration by an average of 2 days. Nasal saline irrigation with high-volume devices (e.g., Neti pot, squeeze bottle) reduces mucosal edema, clears debris, and improves quality of life. Mucolytics (guaifenesin), oral decongestants (pseudoephedrine), and topical decongestants (oxymetazoline, limited to 3 days) provide symptomatic relief. Oral antihistamines are not recommended unless allergic rhinitis is the primary driver.

medicalTreatments.name: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (875/125 mg orally twice daily, 5-7 days in adults)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antibiotic for confirmed bacterial acute rhinosinusitis per IDSA 2012. Covers S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, and M. catarrhalis including beta-lactamase-producing strains. High-dose formulation (2 g/125 mg twice daily) for risk factors for resistant pneumococcus.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure or improvement at 10-14 days in 87-90% of ABRS patients; number needed to treat approximately 15 over placebo in pooled RCTs

medicalTreatments.name: Intranasal corticosteroids (mometasone furoate 200 mcg twice daily or fluticasone 100 mcg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduce mucosal edema and inflammation. Recommended as monotherapy for mild ABRS and adjuncts to antibiotics for moderate-severe disease. Safe for both short- and long-term use; minimal systemic absorption at standard doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce symptom severity scores by 15-25% and shorten duration by approximately 2 days in RCTs (NICE 2017 meta-analysis)

medicalTreatments.name: Saline nasal irrigation (isotonic or hypertonic, high-volume)
medicalTreatments.description: Twice-daily irrigation with 240 mL isotonic saline using squeeze bottle or Neti pot clears mucus, reduces mucosal edema, and improves ciliary function. Hypertonic saline may provide marginally greater symptom relief. Evidence supports use as adjunct to all other therapies.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces symptom burden scores by 30-40% and antibiotic use in systematic reviews of acute and recurrent sinusitis

medicalTreatments.name: Analgesics and antipyretics (ibuprofen 400-600 mg or paracetamol 1 g, every 4-6 hours)
medicalTreatments.description: Provide pain relief for facial pressure and headache. Ibuprofen offers additional anti-inflammatory effect at therapeutic doses. Both are safe for 7-10 days in otherwise healthy adults.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective pain control in 70-80% of patients; no effect on infection resolution but substantially improve quality of life during acute episode

medicalTreatments.name: Topical decongestants (oxymetazoline 0.05% nasal spray, maximum 3 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Provide rapid reduction in nasal obstruction and may transiently improve sinus ostial patency. Restricted to 3 days to avoid rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa). Useful for symptom relief in the first 48-72 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid onset (10-15 min), effective for nasal blockage but no evidence they improve sinusitis cure rates

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline (100 mg twice daily, 5-7 days) or levofloxacin (500 mg once daily, 5 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative antibiotics for penicillin-allergic patients. Doxycycline preferred over fluoroquinolones to preserve fluoroquinolone activity; levofloxacin or moxifloxacin used for severe cases or fluoroquinolone-susceptible pathogens.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical success rates of 85-90% comparable to amoxicillin-clavulanate in RCTs for ABRS

surgicalOptions.name: Functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS)
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic removal of diseased tissue and enlargement of sinus ostia to restore drainage. Reserved for recurrent acute sinusitis (4+ episodes per year) unresponsive to medical therapy, or for complications of acute sinusitis. Performed under general anesthesia, typically as a day procedure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Subjective symptom improvement in 85-90% of carefully selected patients at 12 months; revision surgery needed in 10-15% over 5 years

surgicalOptions.name: Maxillary sinus washout (antral lavage)
surgicalOptions.description: Saline irrigation of the maxillary sinus via a trocar under the inferior turbinate or through the canine fossa. Used diagnostically and therapeutically in refractory maxillary sinusitis to obtain culture material and deliver antibiotics directly into the sinus.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolves retained secretions in 80-90% of cases; predominantly a diagnostic and adjunct therapeutic procedure rather than definitive treatment

alternativeTreatments.name: Xylitol-containing nasal irrigation
alternativeTreatments.description: Xylitol inhibits bacterial adhesion to nasal epithelium. A small RCT (Weissman et al. 2011) found xylitol irrigation improved symptoms compared with saline alone, though evidence remains preliminary.

alternativeTreatments.name: Bromelain supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: A proteolytic enzyme extracted from pineapple; two small RCTs found bromelain (500 mg/day) reduced nasal swelling and pain duration in sinusitis compared with placebo. Not included in major guidelines but considered low risk.

whySeeSpecialist: ENT referral is warranted for four or more episodes of acute sinusitis per year, failure of two antibiotic courses, suspected orbital or intracranial complications, or when imaging suggests structural pathology such as nasal polyps, deviated septum, or anatomical ostial narrowing. An allergist is appropriate when allergic rhinitis co-exists and is suspected to be driving recurrent episodes.
doctorSelectionGuide: For uncomplicated acute sinusitis, a primary care physician or general practitioner is sufficient. Choose an ENT (otolaryngologist) for recurrent or complicated sinusitis; look for a surgeon who performs high-volume FESS with image-guidance systems and who has access to same-day CT reporting. For children, a pediatric ENT or pediatric pulmonologist with ENT training is preferred.
hospitalCriteria: Suspected orbital or intracranial complication (periorbital edema, proptosis, diplopia, meningism, altered consciousness)
Severe sepsis or signs of systemic bacteraemia requiring IV antibiotics
Immunocompromised patient with rapidly progressive sinusitis requiring IV antifungal or broad-spectrum antibiotics
Post-operative acute sinusitis following prior sinus surgery with orbital wall dehiscence
keyFacilities: ENT outpatient clinics with nasal endoscopy facilities
Hospitals with same-day CT capability and ENT/neurosurgery on call for complications
Allergy clinics for skin prick testing and immunotherapy in recurrent sinusitis
Day surgery units for FESS under general anesthesia
preventionStrategies: Wash hands frequently during cold season and avoid touching the nose and eyes — rhinovirus spreads primarily via hand-to-mucosa contact.
Keep allergic rhinitis well controlled with intranasal corticosteroids and/or allergen immunotherapy to reduce mucosal inflammation that predisposes to sinus infection.
Stay up to date with influenza and pneumococcal vaccinations, particularly for children, older adults, and the immunocompromised, to reduce viral and bacterial triggers.
Avoid cigarette smoke (active and passive) which impairs mucociliary clearance and increases colonization with sinusitis pathogens.
Use a humidifier or nasal saline spray during dry-air winter months to prevent mucosal desiccation that impairs drainage.
Treat dental infections promptly to prevent odontogenic maxillary sinusitis, particularly for upper molar root pathology.
lifestyleModifications: Irrigate nasal passages with isotonic or hypertonic saline twice daily during and after an acute episode to maintain drainage and reduce inflammation.
Sleep with the head of the bed elevated 30-45 degrees to improve sinus drainage and reduce nocturnal postnasal drip.
Stay well hydrated (at least 2 L of water per day) to keep mucus secretions thin and easier to clear.
Avoid alcohol and antihistamines (first-generation) which thicken secretions and impair mucociliary clearance.
Use a topical decongestant spray (oxymetazoline) for a maximum of 3 consecutive days only — beyond this duration rebound congestion worsens nasal obstruction.
Apply warm facial compresses over the affected sinus for 10-15 minutes several times daily to relieve facial pain and promote drainage.

recommended: Warm fluids (herbal tea, broth, warm water with honey and lemon) help thin secretions and provide soothing relief
Spicy foods containing capsaicin (chili, horseradish) act as natural decongestants and may transiently improve sinus drainage
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, berries, bell peppers) support immune function during active infection
Zinc-containing foods (pumpkin seeds, oysters, legumes) may modestly shorten viral upper respiratory infection duration
avoid: Alcohol, which causes vasodilation and mucosal congestion and impairs immune response
Dairy products in sensitive individuals — while evidence for mucus-thickening is weak, some patients report subjective worsening
Cold beverages, which some patients find worsen sinus pain and facial pressure during acute flares
Highly processed or sugary foods that may blunt immune response during the acute phase

exerciseGuidelines: Light activity such as walking is generally well tolerated. Avoid vigorous exercise during the acute febrile phase as it can worsen headache and facial pain. Return to normal activity as symptoms improve; swimming should be avoided until fully recovered because chlorine exposure can irritate inflamed mucous membranes.
dailyManagement: Perform nasal saline irrigation with a squeeze bottle morning and evening — flush at least 240 mL per side.
Use prescribed intranasal corticosteroid spray consistently; spray into each nostril aiming toward the outer ear, not up the midline.
Take analgesics on a scheduled basis (every 4-6 hours) rather than waiting for pain to peak, for the first 3-5 days.
Monitor for red flag symptoms — worsening eye swelling, visual changes, severe headache, or neck stiffness — and seek emergency care immediately if any occur.
Maintain fluid intake above 2 L per day and keep indoor humidity at 40-50% with a humidifier.
If taking antibiotics, complete the full course even if symptoms improve early to prevent recurrence and resistance.
prognosis: Acute sinusitis has an excellent prognosis in the vast majority of patients. Viral sinusitis resolves spontaneously within 7-10 days in over 90% of cases. Bacterial acute rhinosinusitis responds to appropriate antibiotics within 72 hours in 85-90% of patients, with complete resolution by 10-14 days. Fewer than 5% of cases develop complications, and serious orbital or intracranial complications occur in approximately 1 in 10,000 cases of bacterial sinusitis. Recurrent acute sinusitis — four or more episodes per year — affects about 20% of sinusitis patients and is associated with an underlying structural or allergic factor. Functional endoscopic sinus surgery resolves recurrence in 85-90% of surgical candidates. Prognosis is worse in immunocompromised patients, in whom refractory fungal or atypical bacterial infection can cause orbital and intracranial spread with significant morbidity.
recoveryTimeline: Viral sinusitis: symptom peak at 48-72 hours, resolution expected by day 7-10. Bacterial sinusitis treated with antibiotics: noticeable improvement within 72 hours, full resolution in 10-14 days. Residual nasal congestion or reduced smell may persist 2-4 weeks after bacterial sinusitis. Complete restoration of mucociliary function takes 4-8 weeks after a severe bacterial episode.
complications: Orbital cellulitis or abscess — extension of ethmoid or maxillary infection into the orbit; presents as periorbital swelling, proptosis, and restricted eye movement; requires urgent CT, IV antibiotics, and often surgical drainage.

Intracranial extension — meningitis, epidural abscess, subdural empyema, or cavernous sinus thrombosis from frontal or sphenoid sinusitis; mortality risk even with treatment makes early recognition critical.
Pott's puffy tumor — subperiosteal abscess of the frontal bone from frontal sinusitis; presents as doughy forehead swelling; treated with surgical debridement plus IV antibiotics.

Chronic sinusitis — persistence of inflammation beyond 12 weeks; affects approximately 5-10% of acute sinusitis patients who do not receive appropriate treatment or who have persistent structural obstruction.
Worsening of co-existing asthma — sinusitis consistently worsens asthma control through the unified airway pathway; unresolved sinusitis predicts more frequent asthma exacerbations.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery (AAO-HNS)
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on sinusitis symptoms, treatments, and when to see a specialist, from the main US ENT professional society.

supportResources.name: CDC — Sinus Infection (Sinusitis)
supportResources.description: CDC guidance on when antibiotics are appropriate for sinusitis and how to manage symptoms effectively at home.

supportResources.name: NICE Clinical Knowledge Summary — Sinusitis
supportResources.description: UK guideline-based clinical and patient information covering diagnosis, treatment, and referral criteria for acute and chronic sinusitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic Rhinitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic rhinitis causes watery, clear discharge, sneezing, and itchy eyes — symptoms that are bilateral and triggered by allergen exposure. Acute sinusitis produces purulent discharge, facial pain, and fever, and follows a viral upper respiratory infection rather than allergen exposure. Allergy testing distinguishes the two when presentations overlap.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Pharyngitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pharyngitis causes sore throat with tonsillar inflammation as the dominant complaint, whereas sinusitis causes facial pressure and purulent nasal discharge. Postnasal drip from sinusitis can cause throat irritation, but absence of facial tenderness and sinus opacification on CT argues against sinusitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Common Cold (Acute Nasopharyngitis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: The common cold causes watery nasal discharge that clears within 7-10 days, without significant facial pain or purulence. Acute sinusitis is diagnosed when symptoms persist beyond 10 days, worsen after initial improvement, or include severe unilateral facial pain with purulent discharge.

confusedWithConditions.name: Deviated Nasal Septum
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A deviated septum causes chronic unilateral nasal obstruction and mouth breathing but not acute facial pain, fever, or purulent discharge. It is a predisposing structural factor for sinusitis rather than a cause of infection itself.

relatedConditions.name: Common Cold
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common antecedent of acute sinusitis; viral upper respiratory infections cause mucosal edema that obstructs sinus drainage in virtually all ABRS cases.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Pharyngitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Pharyngitis frequently co-occurs with sinusitis during upper respiratory infection; postnasal drip from sinusitis can directly cause pharyngeal irritation.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Tonsillitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Tonsillitis and sinusitis share bacterial pathogens (S. pneumoniae, group A Streptococcus) and commonly occur together during upper respiratory infections in children.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Bronchitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Postnasal drip from sinusitis is a major cause of persistent cough; acute bronchitis and sinusitis frequently co-exist as parts of a widespread upper and lower respiratory infection.

faqs.question: How do I know if my sinus infection is bacterial or viral?
faqs.answer: Bacterial sinusitis is likely if symptoms persist beyond 10 days without improvement, if you notice a double-worsening pattern (improving then suddenly worse around day 5-6), or if you have severe fever above 39°C with purulent discharge and unilateral facial pain from day one. Viral sinusitis typically peaks at 48-72 hours and improves by day 7-10.

faqs.question: Do I need antibiotics for a sinus infection?
faqs.answer: No, in most cases. Over 90% of acute sinusitis cases are viral and resolve without antibiotics. Watchful waiting for 10 days is recommended for uncomplicated cases. Antibiotics are indicated only for confirmed bacterial sinusitis — persistent symptoms beyond 10 days, double-worsening, or severe onset with fever above 39°C.

faqs.question: How long does acute sinusitis last?
faqs.answer: Viral sinusitis typically resolves within 7-10 days. Bacterial sinusitis treated with antibiotics improves within 72 hours and fully resolves in 10-14 days. Untreated bacterial sinusitis can persist for 3-4 weeks. If symptoms last longer than 12 weeks, the condition is reclassified as chronic sinusitis.

faqs.question: What is the best antibiotic for a sinus infection?
faqs.answer: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (875/125 mg twice daily for 5-7 days) is the first-line antibiotic per IDSA 2012 guidelines. It covers the main bacterial causes including beta-lactamase-producing strains. Doxycycline or levofloxacin are used if penicillin allergy is present.

faqs.question: Can sinusitis cause tooth pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Maxillary sinusitis commonly causes pain in the upper molar and premolar teeth because their roots lie close to or within the maxillary sinus floor. This odontogenic-type pain can be the dominant complaint. It resolves when the sinus infection clears, distinguishing it from true dental pathology.

faqs.question: Can a sinus infection go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Viral sinusitis resolves without treatment in over 90% of cases within 7-10 days. Most cases of bacterial sinusitis also resolve spontaneously, though more slowly — within 2-4 weeks. The IDSA recommends watchful waiting for uncomplicated presentations because antibiotics provide modest benefit and carry side-effect risks.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs that sinusitis is serious?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care for swelling around the eye, restricted eye movement, vision changes, high fever with confusion or neck stiffness, or severe frontal headache. These suggest orbital or intracranial spread — rare but serious complications requiring CT imaging and urgent treatment.

faqs.question: Does blowing your nose make sinusitis worse?
faqs.answer: Forceful nose-blowing can push bacteria from the nasal cavity into the sinuses via negative pressure. Blow one nostril at a time with gentle pressure, or use saline irrigation instead. Excessive blowing can worsen mucosal irritation and prolong symptoms.

faqs.question: Is a CT scan necessary for sinusitis?
faqs.answer: CT is not needed for a straightforward first episode. It is recommended for recurrent sinusitis (4+ episodes per year), failure of two antibiotic courses, suspected structural causes like polyps or deviated septum, or suspected orbital and intracranial complications. Plain sinus X-rays are no longer recommended due to poor accuracy.

faqs.question: How does saline irrigation help sinusitis?
faqs.answer: High-volume saline irrigation mechanically flushes mucus and debris from the nasal passages, reduces mucosal edema, and improves ciliary function. Systematic reviews show it reduces symptom scores by 30-40% and decreases antibiotic use in both acute and recurrent sinusitis. It is safe and can be used multiple times daily.

faqs.question: Can sinusitis cause loss of smell?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hyposmia (reduced smell) or anosmia (absent smell) occurs in 60-70% of acute sinusitis episodes due to edema blocking the olfactory cleft. Smell typically returns fully within 2-4 weeks as inflammation resolves. Persistent anosmia beyond 4 weeks warrants ENT evaluation for polyps or other structural causes.

faqs.question: Can children get acute sinusitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children aged 2-6 years are particularly susceptible because of frequent viral upper respiratory infections and developing sinus anatomy. Bacterial sinusitis in children presents similarly to adults but requires longer antibiotic courses (10-14 days). Persistent symptoms and recurrent episodes warrant pediatric ENT referral.

faqs.question: What is the difference between acute and chronic sinusitis?
faqs.answer: Acute sinusitis lasts fewer than 4 weeks and is usually infectious. Chronic sinusitis persists beyond 12 weeks and is driven by persistent inflammation, often with polyps, biofilms, or structural obstruction rather than active infection. Chronic sinusitis requires a different management approach including long-term topical steroids and often surgery.

faqs.question: Does exercise help or hurt sinusitis?
faqs.answer: Light walking is generally safe and may help by increasing mucociliary clearance and improving nasal drainage. Vigorous exercise during the febrile phase can worsen headache and facial pain. Avoid swimming during acute sinusitis because chlorine exposure irritates inflamed mucosa. Return to full activity as symptoms resolve.

faqs.question: Is sinusitis related to asthma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Asthma and sinusitis co-occur in 20-40% of patients through shared airway inflammatory pathways. Sinusitis worsens asthma control, and treating sinusitis improves asthma outcomes in approximately 70% of affected patients. Both conditions should be evaluated and managed together in patients with recurrent respiratory disease.

metaTitle: Acute Sinusitis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Acute sinusitis: short-term inflammation of the sinuses causing facial pain and congestion. Symptoms, causes, diagnosis and treatment reviewed.
